verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: The whole subject of the Apocalypse is so treated, in the Speaker's Commentary, as to elucidate many questions suggested by the primitive commentators of this series, and to furnish the latest judgments of critics on the subject.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: It is so immense a matter, however, as to render annotations on patristic specialties impossible in a work like this.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: Every reader must feel how apposite is the sententious saying of Augustine: “Apocalypsis Joannis tot sacramenta quot verba.”

verses.text: It supplements the appearances of the risen Redeemer for identification, by a manifestation, which is the Church’s assurance of His glorification, and of His perpetual work in her and for her, as well as of His presence with her, by the Spirit.

verses.text: He knows of nothing but a place for those “who die in the Lord,” and a place for the wicked.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: It is perpetually overlooked, that, in the fiction of “purgatory,” it is only the righteous who are entitled to it: none but those dying in full communion with the Church having any portion in it, or any title to Masses for their repose.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: It is quite important to observe the voice of antiquity on a matter which, in our own times, has been made a stumbling-block to souls by a wanton, personal act of the Bishop of Rome and his dogma of the “Immaculate Conception.”

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: To make our author consistent with himself (see note 10, supra), we should read thus: “But they have in their darkness a light (some think) such as the moon.”

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: Here, however, it seems to me, he is giving his mind to “the Church of Fathers and Prophets” exclusively, in which its “saints and apostles” were for a time waiting and looking for the Man-child.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: Even that Church of the Hebrews had, in Hades, light “like that of the moon,” where they reposed in Abraham’s bosom; but Christ removed them into a fairer region, i.e., Paradise, when He illuminated Hades, and then became “the first-fruits of them that slept.”

verses.text: Indeed, if the Victorinus mentioned by Jerome be the same as our author, the mention of Genseric proves the subsequent interpolation of his works.

verses.book: Commentary on the Apocalypse of the Blessed John
verses.text: It is evident that the fragment which is here preserved, if, indeed, it be the work of Caius Marius Victorinus, surnamed Afer, is full of the corrections of some pious disciple of St. Augustine who lived much later.

verses.text: But the great probability that there were two authors of the name living in different ages seems more than suspected by the learned.

verses.text: Dupin, who calls him Marius without the Caius (changed to Fabius by the English translator), leaves one yet more in a mist as to the identity of our author with the one he writes about.
